content:
You are a research assistant who answers questions through iterative reasoning and research.

## Process
- Use <think></think> tags to show your reasoning at any point.
- Use the provided tools when you need information.
- You can alternate between thinking and searching multiple times.
- Only provide <answer></answer> tags when you have enough information for a complete response. If the problem asks for a specific, short-form answer, you can also put the answer string in the \boxed{} format. 
- Support every non-trivial claim with retrieved evidence. Wrap the exact claim span in <cite id="ID1,ID2">...</cite>, where id are snippet IDs from searched results (comma-separated if multiple). Use only returned snippets; never invent IDs. Avoid citing filler text - cite just the factual claim.

## Answer and Citation Format

- Once you collect all of the necessary information, generate the final answer, and mark your answer with answer tags: <answer></answer>. 
- If your answer is short (e.g., a phrase or a number), you can also put the answer string in the \boxed{} format.
- In your answer, wrap the supported text in <cite id="SNIPPET_ID"> ... </cite>. You have to use the exact ID from a returned <snippet id=...>...</snippet>.
- If multiple sources support a passage, use multiple <cite> tags around the relevant clauses/sentences.
- Examples 
<cite id="S17">LLMs often hallucinate on long-tail facts.</cite>
<answer>Based on the search results, <cite id="S23">the first Harry Potter movie was released on November 16, 2001.</cite>Therefore, the final answer is \boxed{November 16, 2001}.</answer>

## WORKFLOW EXAMPLE

Below is a simple example that demonstrates the process and the correct use of tools and tags. In practice, you'll often need additional search iterations, and your final answer may be much longer (e.g., a multi-paragraph report).

Question: Give a concise update on 2024 renewable energy market trends and current commercial solar efficiency benchmarks. 

<think>I need to understand the current market trends first</think>
[call google_search with appropriate arguments]
[tool results returned]

<think>Now I need specific data on solar panel efficiency</think>
[call snippet_search with appropriate arguments]
[tool results returned]

<think>I have enough to answer succinctly</think>
<answer>
Global renewables expanded rapidly in 2024, <cite ids="S_p0Zr41Q,S_GxA2ZLh">driven primarily by the growth of solar and wind energy.</cite> 
<cite ids="S_x4xU7dU">State-of-the-art commercial solar modules report cell efficiencies of ~26-27% and module efficiencies of ~23-24%.</cite>
\boxed{Solar leads 2024 renewables; top commercial module efficiency ~ 23-24%}
</answer>

## REQUIREMENTS
- Think and search iteratively until you have sufficient information
- Only provide the final answer when ready
- Cite all claims from search results using exact snippet IDs

function.name: google_search
function.description: General web search. Returns web search snippets.

function.name: browse_webpage
function.description: Open a specific URL and extract readable page text as snippets.

function.name: snippet_search
function.description: Focused snippet retrieval from scientific papers.

year.description: Publication year; single number (e.g. 2024) or range (e.g. 2022-2025).

fieldsOfStudy.description: One or comma-separated list of fields of study.

content:
According to the Wikipedia article on Academic publishing, what dispute rate did Robert K. Merton report for simultaneous discoveries in the 17th century?

For the given question, please think and search iteratively to find the answer, and provide the final answer in following format: <answer>\boxed{exact answer}</answer>.

<think>Goal: Find the specific dispute rate reported by Robert K. Merton for simultaneous discoveries in the 17th century, as stated in Wikipedia's "Academic publishing" article.

Plan:
- Use Google search to find the "Academic publishing" Wikipedia page.
- Browse the page and search within it for "Merton" and "simultaneous discoveries" to extract the exact dispute rate and century reference.
- If needed, cross-check via page anchor or history section.

First query: google_search for "Wikipedia Academic publishing Merton dispute rate simultaneous discoveries 17th century".</think>
function_calls:
<tool_call>
<function=google_search>
<parameter=num>5</parameter>
<parameter=gl>us</parameter>
<parameter=hl>en</parameter>
<parameter=query>Wikipedia Academic publishing Merton dispute rate simultaneous discoveries 17th century</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

arguments.query: Wikipedia Academic publishing Merton dispute rate simultaneous discoveries 17th century

content:
<snippet id="S_xtVhZmB">
Title: Academic publishing
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academic_publishing
Text: ... simultaneous discovery in the 17th century ended in dispute. The number of disputes dropped to 72% in the 18th century, 59% by the latter half of the 19th ...</snippet>
<snippet id="S_Fo9Aqt0">
Title: Multiple discovery
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiple_discovery
Text: The hypothesis that most scientific discoveries and inventions are made independently and more or less simultaneously by multiple scientists and inventors.</snippet>
<snippet id="S_Purwp4F">
Title: Merton thesis
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Merton_thesis
Text: The Merton thesis is an argument about the nature of early experimental science proposed by Robert K. Merton.</snippet>
<snippet id="S_PbyLp2g">
Title: List of multiple discoveries
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_multiple_discoveries
Text: Merton contrasted a "multiple" with a "singleton"—a discovery that has been made uniquely by a single scientist or group of scientists working together. The ...</snippet>
<snippet id="S_xV7Im0o">
Title: Merton, Robert K 1957 "Priorities in Scientific Discovery
URL: https://www.academia.edu/34402380/Merton_Robert_K_1957_Priorities_in_Scientific_Discovery
Text: The paper explores the interplay between science as a social institution and the significance of priority disputes in scientific discovery.</snippet>

<think>The first Google result directly points to the Wikipedia page "Academic publishing" and its snippet mentions dispute rates by century but truncates the 17th-century value. I need to open the page and find the exact rate attributed to Robert K. Merton. Next step: browse the Wikipedia page and search within for "Merton" and "dispute" to extract the 17th-century percentage.</think>

<snippet id="S_UD6WfAE">
Title: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academic_publishing
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academic_publishing
Text: Robert K. Merton found that 92% of cases of simultaneous discovery in the 17th century ended in dispute.</snippet>

<think>The browse result from the Academic publishing Wikipedia page explicitly states: "Robert K. Merton found that 92% of cases of simultaneous discovery in the 17th century ended in dispute," which directly answers the question. This is sufficient; no further verification is needed for this specific query. I will provide the minimal short-form answer.</think>
\boxed{92%}
